retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Etiology -- Viral Hepatitis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic hepatitis B virus and chronic hepatitis C virus is associated with more than 70% of cases of hepatocellular carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Etiology -- Viral Hepatitis
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis B virus- Hepatitis B virus (HBV) is an enveloped virus, partially double-stranded virus, circular DNA genome, and belonging to the family Hepadnavirus . Hepatitis B affects more than 250 million individuals worldwide and is the most common cause of chronic hepatitis worldwide. Integration of the hepatitis B virus genome into the host genome is the primary pathogenesis for oncogenesis in HBV. Insertion of viral genome in telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT) promoter sites of the human genome resulting in mutation accounting for 60% of HCC cases. [4] Other genetic alterations include mutations in TP53 (affecting cell cycle), beta-1 catenin (CTNNBI), axis inhibitor-1 (AXINI), AT-rich interaction domain-containing protein 1A (ARID1A), and ARID2(chromatin proliferation). The Hepatitis B virus and the Hepatitis C virus infection accounts for 56 % and 20% of HCC cases diagnosed worldwide, respectively. [5] HCC can occur in the absence of cirrhosis in patients infected with the hepatitis B virus infection. More than 80% of HBV-related HCC have underlying cirrhosis. [6] A strong risk predictor for hepatocellular carcinoma in patients with HBV includes elevated serum HBV DNA levels (equal to or more than 10,000 copies/mL). [7] This is independent of the hepatitis B e antigen (HBeAg) status of the patient. [8] Also, the positivity of the hepatitis B e antigen is associated with an increased incidence of HCC. This may be an indicator of a prolonged replication phase. HBV genotype C is associated with an increased risk for HCC. [9] Patients with low hepatitis B virus load but high levels of hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) with levels of more than 1000 IU/mL are significantly associated with HCC. [10] Viral coinfection with the hepatitis C virus and the hepatitis delta virus increases the risk for the development of HCC. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The Sorafenib Hepatocellular Carcinoma Assessment Randomized Protocol (SHARP) study, a multicenter, phase 3, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with 602 patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma compared sorafenib (at a dose of 400 mg twice per day) to placebo and results revealed time to progression of 5.5 months versus 2.8 months and mOS of 10.7 months versus 7.9 months, in favor of sorafenib and statistically significant for both outcomes. Of note, the SHARP study only included hepatocellular carcinoma patients with Child-Pugh A, and a subgroup analysis showed HCV positive patients had a longer median survival of 14 months. Sorafenib was for long the only approved hepatocellular carcinoma option in the advanced disease setting until the Regorafenib After Sorafenib in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma (RESORCE) study with regorafenib (a multikinase inhibitor) revealed positive results. This was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter phase 3, that enrolled 573 patients with Hepatocellular carcinoma whose disease has progressed after treatment with sorafenib greater than 400 mg/day and CP-A (97%). The trial compared 160 mg regorafenib once daily for 3 weeks on and 1 week off, or best supportive care plus placebo, with 28 days cycles. The results revealed a statistically significant median of survival of 10.6 months in the regorafenib group when compared with 7.8 months in the control arm. The Regorafenib After Sorafenib in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma (RESORCE) study included 87% of patients with hepatocellular carcinoma and a Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer system stage C. Another option for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma whose disease progressed on sorafenib therapy is ramucirumab (a recombinant monoclonal antibody which inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2). In the REACH study, a median of survival was improved with ramucirumab (9.2 months) compared with placebo (7.6 months) but did not reach statistical significant (p = 0.1391). Nonetheless, a sub-analysis demonstrated a significant median of survival benefit for patients with an AFP level of 400 ng/mL or greater and Child-Pugh A for 7.8 months for ramucirumab compared with 4.2 months for placebo (HR, 0.67; 95% CI; 0.51, 0.90). REACH-2 study of ramucirumab in patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma with underlying Child-Pugh A cirrhosis and baseline AFP levels of 400 ng/mL or more is currently recruiting to evaluate this hypothesis. Ongoing clinical trials and future therapies are focused on immunotherapy (nivolumab and durvalumab) and small molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitors (cabozantinib). [15] [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of the Hepatitis C virus include: End-stage liver disease Liver cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Universal hepatitis B vaccination is likely to decrease the incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma worldwide. Direct antiviral for the treatment of chronic hepatitis C cirrhosis and the achievement of sustained viral response is likely to decrease the incidence of HCC. Non-cirrhotic chronic hepatitis B virus infection with high-risk for the development of HCC include patients with elevated ALT and high serum hepatitis B virus DNA, family history of HCC, patients older than age 20 years from sub-Saharan Africa, Asian men and women older than 40 and 50 years should undergo surveillance. [51] Surveillance with ultrasound and with or without alpha-fetoprotein every 4-6 months is likely to increase the detection of early HCC.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Five-year survival of hepatocellular carcinoma is 18% and second to pancreatic cancer. The prognosis of patients with HCC is dependent on the tumor size, differentiation or grade of the tumor on histopathology, severity of the underlying liver disease, presence or absence of metastases, and tumor extension to adjacent structures. HCC with high levels of alpha-fetoprotein is associated with poorly differentiated HCC and confers a poor prognosis. [65] Patients with hepatitis B virus-related HCC and positive serum hepatitis B e antigen(HBeAg) are associated with poor prognosis and higher recurrence of HCC. [66] [67] High levels of serum hepatitis B virus DNA are associated with a higher risk for HCC and recurrence. [68] [69] Diabetes mellitus is a risk factor for the development of HCC and also associated with a poor prognosis. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis B, hepatitis C, alcoholic liver disease, and non-alcoholic liver steatohepatitis/non-alcoholic fatty liver disease are the etiological factors for the development of hepatocellular carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who develop hepatocellular carcinoma may be candidates for locally directed therapies (e.g., radiofrequency ablation, transarterial chemoembolization), systemic chemotherapy, or surgical resection in carefully selected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with chronic HBV infection are at risk of developing hepatocellular carcinoma, even in the absence of cirrhosis. Hepatitis B-associated hepatocellular carcinoma is responsible for 45% of primary liver cancer worldwide. About 1% of patients can also develop fulminant hepatic failure, and the mortality rate is approximately 80% in those patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Etiology -- Viral Hepatitis
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis C virus- Hepatitis C virus (HCV) is a partially double-stranded, plus-sense RNA virus with 11 major genotypes, and 15 different subtypes. HCV genotype 1b is frequently associated with HCC. [12] [13] HCV does not integrate with the host genome. Cirrhosis is a significant step in viral carcinogenesis for HCC. Chronic inflammation in chronic hepatitis C virus infection with subsequent fibrosis, necrosis, and regeneration contributes to HCC development. Molecular markers noted in liver carcinogenesis include viral structural and non-structural proteins (NS3, NS4A, NS4B, NS5A, and NS5B). HCV-associated HCC mostly occurs in patients with cirrhosis or advanced stages of fibrosis. [14] Fewer cases of HCV-related HCC have been documented in patients without cirrhosis. [15] 20% of HCC cases diagnosed worldwide are caused by HCV. Viral coinfection with the hepatitis B virus is associated with increased risk for HCC. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Hepatocellular Carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: HCC is the most common primary cancer in the liver, and its incidence is increasing. [32] Cirrhosis secondary to HBV and HCV is the most common risk factor. [32] Routine monitoring of cirrhotic patients for the development of HCC is recommended, with at least 6 monthly screenings using abdominal ultrasonography. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Persons with end-stage renal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Only 10-15% of patients infected with HCV have a self-limited infection; in all others, the infection is progressive. About 20% develop cirrhosis within 2 decades, and another 1% to 5% develop liver cancer within 3 decades. The progression of the disease is more common in heavy users of alcohol, cirrhotics, and those with a coexisting HBV infection. Individuals who have an undetectable viral load generally have a decreased risk of developing cirrhosis and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammation in chronic hepatitis C virus infection with subsequent fibrosis, necrosis, and regeneration contributes to HCC development. Molecular markers noted in liver carcinogenesis include viral structural and non-structural proteins (NS3, NS4A, NS4B, NS5A, and NS5B). HCV-associated HCC mostly occurs in patients with cirrhosis or advanced stages of fibrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Chemoembolization -- Preparation -- Biopsy Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A liver lesion is suspected of malignancy but does not meet imaging criteria for HCC (eg, defined by the American College of Radiology Liver Reporting and Data System). A liver lesion meets imaging criteria for HCC, but the patient has any of the following: Is not considered to have any risk factors for HCC, eg, cirrhosis or chronic hepatitis Has risk factors for cholangiocarcinoma instead of HCC, eg, elevated serum CA 19-9 or carcinoembryonic antigen level Is part of a research trial in which histologic grading or molecular characterization is needed

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct is a malignant condition that manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell cancer). Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد), which is classified as liver inflammation, is clinically associated with سرطان الخلايا الكبدية, indicating that patients with hepatitis may have an increased risk of developing this type of liver cancer. Moreover, سرطان الخلايا الكبدية has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, a jaundiced liver disease, suggesting that liver cell cancer can be part of or contribute to the clinical spectrum of jaundice‑related hepatic disorders. Recognizing the interconnections among cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct, hepatitis, and مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ is essential for comprehensive diagnosis and management of hepatic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2620
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic hepatitis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a liver disorder caused by excessive alcohol consumption that can progress to hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). This relationship highlights the potential evolution from an inflammatory, potentially reversible condition to an irreversible stage of extensive liver scarring, emphasizing the importance of early detection and treatment of alcoholic hepatitis to prevent the development of cirrhosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) is classified as liver inflammation. It is clinically associated with both alcohol‑abstinence syndrome (متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول) and hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية). Hepatocellular carcinoma is a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct and also has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). These interconnections highlight the role of hepatitis in the development of liver cancer and related hepatic disorders, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing hepatitis to prevent progression to malignancy and other liver complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: Telaprevir is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of drugs used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 638
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, and telaprevir is a member of this antiviral group used specifically to treat hepatitis C virus infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 369
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, making it a member of the therapeutic group “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1262
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, referred to as “játra” in Czech, is the anatomical site for a range of diagnostic procedures, diseases, and therapeutic interventions. The procedure known in English as “biopsy of liver” is classified by the Arabic term “خزعة الكبد,” and this partially excised‑anatomy procedure is performed on the liver (“játra”). Hepatitis, identified in Arabic as “اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد,” also localizes to the liver and is clinically associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (“سرطان الخلايا الكبدية”). Hepatocellular carcinoma represents a manifestation of “cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct,” and it has a broader relationship to the disease category “مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ.” In addition to these liver‑specific conditions, the liver serves as an indirect site for other non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections” comprises agents such as ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as antiviral medications specifically to manage hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (hepatocellular carcinoma). Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَبُ الكَبِد) is clinically associated with سرطان الخلايا الكبدية, indicating that chronic liver inflammation can predispose to hepatocellular carcinoma. Alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) is clinically associated with اِلْتِهَبُ الكَبِد, highlighting alcohol use as a risk factor for liver inflammation. سرطان الخلايا الكبدية also has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, suggesting that hepatocellular carcinoma may be linked to cholestatic or jaundiced liver disease. The term hepatitis classifies اِلْتِهَبُ الكَبِد, confirming the equivalence of the English and Arabic nomenclature. Understanding these interconnections is important for patient care, as alcohol dependence can lead to hepatitis, which in turn increases the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma and may be associated with broader cholestatic liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are direct acting antivirals, indicating that these medications specifically target viral proteins essential for hepatitis C virus replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1074
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram is an antidepressant medication; it is not treated by alcohol dependence, and the suggestion that alcohol dependence could serve as therapy for citalopram is inaccurate. Alcohol dependence, however, is clinically associated with inflammation of the liver (hepatitis), and this relationship can exacerbate liver pathology. Hepatitis is classified as inflammation of the liver (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) and represents a key clinical condition that links several liver‑related diseases. Hepatitis is also clinically associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), a specific manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile ducts. In turn, hepatocellular carcinoma has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), reflecting its place within the spectrum of serious liver disorders. Understanding these interconnections—alcohol dependence contributing to hepatitis, hepatitis predisposing to hepatocellular carcinoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma being part of broader cholestatic liver disease—helps clinicians assess risk factors, monitor disease progression, and devise comprehensive management strategies for patients with liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis is the clinical term that classifies the condition known in Arabic as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد, which refers to inflammation of the liver.
